nativeRange.habitat: Wet ground and shallow margins of river flats, swamps, and dams; grows as a creeping mat-forming herb that tolerates both submersed and emersed conditions

decorPreferences: fine substrate that holds tiny roots
unshaded planting position with direct light from above

summary: Glosso (Glossostigma elatinoides) is a classic foreground carpeting plant from Australia and New Zealand, associated with the Amano-style planted aquarium. It is generally considered demanding: high light and CO2 injection are commonly advised, as weak light tends to push the plant upward rather than spreading it into a low carpet.
careNotes: Glossostigma elatinoides is a small creeping herb in the family Phrymaceae, native to eastern Australia (New South Wales, Victoria, South Australia, Tasmania, and the ACT) and New Zealand, where it occurs on wet river flats and around swamps and dams. In the aquarium hobby it is one of the classic foreground carpeting plants popularized by Takashi Amano. Lighting is the load-bearing parameter — strong light is essential, and under weak light the plant has a well-documented tendency to grow vertically toward the surface ("running to the top") rather than spreading horizontally into a carpet. CO2 injection is widely recommended for tight, dense carpets; thin pale leaves and yellowing are commonly reported signs of nutrient or CO2 deficiency. A nutrient-rich aquasoil substrate is generally advised, since the tiny roots can be difficult to anchor in coarse gravel and the plant is a heavy feeder. Plant by separating tissue-cultured or potted clumps into small plugs with tweezers and pressing each plug into the substrate. Once established, the plant spreads by creeping runners; trim regularly to maintain even carpet height — older lower layers will rot if shaded by overgrowth above them.
breedingNotes: Propagation is vegetative: the plant produces horizontal creeping runners (stolons) that root at the nodes to form a carpet. Mature carpets are propagated by lifting a section, cutting it into small plugs that include both leaves and roots, and replanting individually with tweezers. Tissue-cultured starter portions and emersed (dry-start / Wabi Kusa) cultivation are both commonly used to establish new carpets.

primary.name: Tropica
primary.notes: Designated primary per Stage A manifest. The Tropica plantdetails page did not return article body text via fetch (only site chrome resolved), so Buce Plant and Modern Aquarium were used as the effective primary care references; Tropica retained as the canonical Tropica entry for this species.

additional.name: Modern Aquarium
additional.notes: Cross-checked common name ("Glosso"), height (~½ in / ~1.3 cm under optimal carpeting), temperature 60–80°F (~15–27°C), CO2 beneficial but not strictly required, strong-light dependence, vertical growth as a low-light failure mode, foreground placement, and propagation by small plugs.

additional.name: Buce Plant
additional.notes: Provided family (Phrymaceae), height (1–2 in / ~2.5–5 cm), pH 6–7.5, CO2 recommended, medium-to-high lighting, moderate-to-fast growth, medium-to-difficult care, propagation by cutting new growth with roots, and tiny-root anchoring difficulty in coarse substrate. New Zealand origin confirmed (alongside Australia per Wikipedia).

additional.name: AquariumPlants.com
additional.notes: Cross-checked native range (Australia and New Zealand), high lighting requirement, foreground placement, fast spreading growth form, and pH 5.5–8 (wider end of the spread). Care rating "easy" was outvoted by Buce Plant (medium-difficult) and Modern Aquarium (moderate) in the averaged careLevel.

additional.name: Wikipedia
additional.notes: Taxonomy confirmation: family Phrymaceae, order Lamiales. Native distribution: New South Wales, Victoria, South Australia, Tasmania, and the Australian Capital Territory; mat-forming perennial herb of wet river flats, swamps, and dams. Common name 'small mud-mat'.
